farLeftText: From 4m ago 11.03 EDT As Troy Jackson becomes frontrunner for Maine Democrats, what happens next? A reminder of the next steps of the frantic replacement process in the Maine Senate race now that Troy Jackson appears poised to clinch the Democratic nomination. Over the weekend, all 16 counties elected 500 delegates who will vote in a special convention in Bangor on Saturday, with the remaining delegates coming from the state Democratic party committee. This will the formal process of selecting the new nominee after Graham Platner abruptly ended his campaign following allegations of sexual assault from a former partner. Jackson, now the progressive frontrunner, once campaigned alongside Platner and received his endorsement during his gubernatorial run, where he finished third. That proximity to Platner – whose campaign was beset by controversy – raised concerns that Jackson might be tainted in the breakneck process to replace the disgraced former nominee. Questions have also lingered about Jackson’s public speaking style and whether he could match the commanding presence that helped Platner build a statewide populist following. But Jackson has consolidated support from major unions, numerous state Democrats, and national progressive groups, and he appears to have locked down broad backing from the newly selected delegates. While their commitments are not legally binding, a large majority of the 601 delegates have now pledged to vote for him. On Sunday, Jackson told Politico it was “incredible” to see people “really concerned about making sure this momentum, this movement continues” as it became clear he would emerge as the presumptive nominee and his rivals dropped out of the competition. His campaign said Monday it was turning its “full focus” to defeating Collins after the weekend’s surge of delegate support. State Democrats have until 27 July to formally choose Platner’s replacement for the general-election ballot. Share Updated at 11.04 EDT 40s ago 11.06 EDT José Olivares One of Mexico’s most important and significant drug traffickers in recent history will be sentenced this morning by a federal judge in Brooklyn and will likely spend the rest of his life behind bars. Ismael Zambada García, the 76-year-old Mexican drug lord known as “El Mayo”, helped co-found and lead the Sinaloa cartel, becoming one of the most wanted targets by US intelligence and law enforcement agencies for decades. El Mayo signed a guilty plea last August and decided not to proceed with a federal trial that would have likely lasted weeks and cost the US government millions of dollars. El Mayo is facing life-in-prison, due to his age, the gravity of his crimes and the guilty plea he signed. The US government is also asking that he pay $15bn as part of his punishment. As one of the co-founders of the Sinaloa cartel, El Mayo is one of the biggest drug traffickers in the history of the US-Mexico drug war. The US government has accused him of trafficking tons of cocaine, marijuana, heroin and, in recent years, fentanyl to the US and other parts of the world. Prosecutors also accused him of being one of the leaders of a violent organization that led to corruption among Mexican politicians, military officials and law enforcement agents. Although Joaquín “El Chapo” Guzmán is known to be the face of the Sinaloa cartel, the elderly Mayo was known to be the elusive leader, who ran the cartel’s operations from behind-the-scenes. El Mayo was little-seen by the public and was relatively unknown, in sharp contrast to El Chapo. El Mayo’s arrest in 2024, however, came as a shock. During a mysterious operation, he was beaten and kidnapped by one of El Chapo’s sons and was flown across the border to New Mexico, where he was arrested by US federal agents. It is still unknown whether US agencies helped facilitate the cross-border kidnapping operation that led to his arrest. El Chapo’s son, who orchestrated the kidnapping, pled guilty late last year and has become a cooperating witness for the US Justice Department. El Mayo’s arrest led to a splintering of the Sinaloa cartel, with El Mayo’s son and El Chapo’s sons leading different factions in a raging inner-cartel war for territory that has ravaged parts of Mexico. El Mayo’s sentence comes amid a difficult time in US-Mexico relations, as the Trump administration has escalated its pressure on the Mexican government to further attack criminal groups. Last year, the Trump administration designated the Sinaloa cartel as a terrorist group and has threatened military intervention to attack the cartel and other drug trafficking organizations. Reporters are heading up to the courtroom now, where the Brooklyn federal judge Brian Cogan, who also presided over El Chapo’s trial years ago, will dictate El Mayo’s sentence. Share Updated at 06.36 EDT 5h ago 06.17 EDT Trump says Qatari-gifted Air Force One will get 'maxed out' upgrades The US president, Donald Trump, has said the Qatari-donated jet that serves ⁠as an Air Force One plane will soon be sent away for “maxed out” upgrades, as concerns grow over the plane’s security features. Trump started flying aboard ⁠the Boeing luxury jet on ⁠1 July ​after accepting the $400m plane as an “unconditional” gift from the Qatari government last year. But concerns about the plane came into focus earlier this month when the president unexpectedly flew from Turkey to England onboard the older Air Force One, rather than the new, Qatari-gifted aircraft he had used to travel to the Nato summit in Ankara. Trump ‌was asked about whether the Qatari plane lacked missile-defense capabilities as he returned home ‌from the World Cup final in New Jersey on Sunday. He told reporters: “Well, it has – it has a lot, and you know it has a lot of capability, but as I understand it, in about a month or so, they’re going to send it to have it be maxed out. So they’re going to be sending it, and they’ll have it be maxed out.” “It’ll take about a month,” Trump added, suggesting the Qatari jet could get additional defensive capabilities to enhance its security. View image in fullscreen Donald Trump disembarks from the Qatar-gifted Air Force One after his first flight on the aircraft, in Bismarck, North Dakota, on 1 July 2026. Photograph: Saul Loeb/AFP/Getty Images Earlier this month, the New York Times reported that the new jet lacks the same defensive countermeasures as the older model, including antimissile capabilities, citing anonymous sources. The coverage prompted a leak investigation and led the Trump administration to issue subpoenas to several of the Times’ journalists in what was condemned as an attempt to punish the outlet for its reporting and to intimidate independent media organisations. The subpoenas, some of which were delivered to reporters at their homes, sought to force the reporters to testify before a federal grand jury in Manhattan. The Times has since filed a motion to quash the subpoenas served by the justice department. Share Updated at 06.18 EDT 5h ago 05.53 EDT Trump administration activates dormant court for ‘alien terrorist’ deportations In other news, the Trump administration has filed a first-ever petition to a secretive and dormant court created 30 years ago to consider government requests to deport so-called “alien terrorists” from the US. The Alien Terrorist Removal Court (ATRC) was established in 1996 but had never received a petition until Wednesday, when the US justice department filed an application seeking the removal of an individual whose name is withheld from the single-page document posted on the court’s website. Joan Ericksen, the chief judge of the five-member court, said in a written response to the petition that a hearing was held on Thursday during which the court had “questions about the nexus that the government alleges between the actions of the respondent and the specific sections and subsections it invokes with respect to those actions”. Ericksen, a federal judge in Minnesota, wrote: “The answers persuaded the court that the government could benefit from the opportunity for more thoughtful consideration.” She directed the justice department to provide more information by Wednesday. Maine Democrats close in on pick to replace Senate candidate Graham Platner Maine Democrats appear to be settling on a frontrunner to replace former Senate candidate Graham Platner in a rushed process to take on Republican Sen. Susan Collins.

Politics Maine Democrats close in on pick to replace Senate candidate Graham Platner Maine Democrats close in on pick to replace Senate candidate Graham Platner Listen · 3:09 3:09 Maine Democrats appear to be settling on a frontrunner to replace former Senate candidate Graham Platner in a rushed process to take on Republican Sen. Susan Collins. Sponsor Message Sponsor Message
centerRightText:
NEW You can now listen to Fox News articles!

Former Maine Senate President Troy Jackson appears to have locked up enough delegates to win the abbreviated race to replace now ex-candidate Graham Platner as the Democratic Senate nominee in the high-stakes showdown to face off with longtime Republican Sen. Susan Collins.

Delegates aligned with Jackson won the vast majority of the 500 slots that were up for grabs on Saturday and Sunday as Maine's 16 counties held two days of delegate selection for next weekend's nominating convention. And Jackson's top two rivals for the nomination, Maine Secretary of State Shenna Bellows and Nirav Shah, a former top public health official, dropped out of the race on Sunday.

"In November, Mainers will defeat Susan Collins, and we will take back our Senate seat from the billionaires and corporations who think they own it," Jackson said in a social media post Sunday evening.

While the delegates on Jackson's slate are not required to support him at next weekend convention, he is in an extremely strong position to secure the nomination and face off in the midterm elections against Collins in what's expected to be a combative and expensive ballot box battle that's among a dozen nationwide which will determine if the Democrats can win back the Senate majority. The GOP currently holds the chamber 53-47.

DEMOCRACY ’26: STAY UP TO DATE WITH THE FOX NEWS ELECTION HUB

A fifth-generation logger and a one-time conservative Republican who became a Democrat over two decades ago, Jackson came in third place last month in Maine's Democratic gubernatorial primary.

On Saturday, in the race to replace Platner, Jackson-aligned delegates won more than 90% of the slots, including all 149 in Cumberland County, Maine's most populous county and home to the state's largest city, Portland.

"Yesterday was an unbelievable huge step, but our work is not finished — not even close," Jackson said Sunday morning. And the winning streak of Jackson-aligned delegates continued throughout the day.

Over 470 of the 500 delegates elected this weekend were on Jackson's slate. The delegates chosen will join another 101 coming automatically from the Democratic State Committee. All 601 will officially vote for the Senate nominee at next Saturday's convention in Bangor, Maine.

Bellows, who unsuccessfully ran for the Democratic gubernatorial nomination, exited the Senate sprint on Sunday morning.

"Donald Trump’s attacks on our democracy are only accelerating, and the best way that I can serve our state and country at this moment is by focusing exclusively on my role as Secretary of State," Bellows said in a statement that didn't mention Jackson.

SIGN UP TO GET THE POLITICS NEWSLETTER

Shah, a former director the Maine Center for Disease Control and Prevention from 2019 to 2023 who later served as principal deputy director of the federal CDC during then-President Joe Biden's administration, and who came in second in last month's Democratic gubernatorial primary, dropped out Sunday evening and backed Jackson.

"To Maine Democrats: Susan Collins will not be defeated without a united Democratic Party. And we cannot unite without treating one another with respect," Shah wrote in a statement. "No matter whom you supported in this race, I urge us all to remember that in the weeks ahead."

Jackson, in a social media post, thanked Shah and urged, "Now it's time for all of us to come together, build the broadest possible coalition, and defeat Susan Collins. Let's get to work."

Jordan Wood, a former congressional staffer for then-Rep. Katie Porter of California who unsuccessfully ran for the Senate as well as the Democratic nomination in the state's 2nd Congressional District this cycle, also dropped out on Sunday. Another Senate candidate, Maine Beer Company co-founder Dan Kleban, announced a campaign suspension on Sunday as well..

MAINE DEMOCRATS RALLY BEHIND PLATNER'S PLATFORM IN SPRINT TO REPLACE HIM

Along with Platner, Jackson had the support of progressive champion Sen. Bernie Sanders of Vermont as he ran for governor, and he is currently backed by 'Our Revolution,' the group born in 2016 out of Sanders' first presidential campaign.

Jackson, similar to Platner, is running on a progressive and populist platform aimed at working-class voters. He backs raising taxes on billionaires, the universal healthcare system known as "Medicare-for-all," abolishing U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement, and he opposes military aid to Israel at a time that Democrats are increasingly divided on support for America's longtime Middle East ally.

Platner, a combat veteran and oyster farmer who was backed by Sanders soon after he launched his bid last August, saw his campaign quickly catch fire. Two-term Democratic Gov. Janet Mills, who was heavily recruited by longtime Democratic Senate leader Sen. Chuck Schumer to run for the Senate, suspended her campaign this spring after trailing Platner in fundraising and in the polls.

THE TWELVE RACES THAT WILL DETERMINE THE SENATE MAJORITY

But even as Platner steamrolled the Democratic establishment and crushed the competition last month to capture the nomination, he had already been forced on defense in the wake of a slew of scandals.

Platner dropped out earlier this month, amid calls from top Democrats in Maine and across the country to immediately exit the race following an explosive report containing an allegation of rape from a woman he previously dated. Platner denied the report but stepped aside.

While Jackson was among those who quickly called on Platner to end his bid in the wake of the rape allegation, he had previously campaigned alongside Platner and made the case that he was the best candidate to continue the fight for the progressive and populist agenda.

"This movement has always been bigger than one person. It's about taking on a system rigged against working people, fighting for ‘Medicare-for-all,’ strong unions, higher wages, reproductive freedom, and an economy where billionaires and corporations finally pay their fair share," Jackson said last week during a Our Revolution virtual town hall, as he pointed to Platner supporters.

CLICK HERE TO DOWNLOAD THE FOX NEWS APP

At last week's first debate among the replacement candidates, Jackson pointed to Platner and said, "That's the thing that I think Graham did the best: talked about fighting against the health care industry and the prescription drug industry to make sure that people in this state and this country have affordable, quality health care."

farLeftText: From 12m ago 10.54 EDT Deputy prime minister David Lammy sacked David Lammy has announced he is also leaving government, having been the deputy prime minister for the past two years. He too posted details of his departure on social media. Writing on X, Lammy said: double quotation mark It has been the greatest privilege of my life, coming from humble beginnings in Tottenham, to serve in government as @Keir_Starmer’s Foreign Secretary, Justice Secretary and Britain’s first Black Deputy Prime Minister. I am immensely proud of what we achieved, in Opposition and in Government. He added: double quotation mark I would have been proud to continue serving in Cabinet. But a new Prime Minister is entitled to build his own team, and I wish @AndyBurnham and the new Cabinet every success. I will continue to serve the great people of Tottenham, support this Labour government from the backbenches and do everything I can to help defeat Reform again at the next general election. Share 6m ago 11.01 EDT Chancellor Rachel Reeves leaves government Rachel Reeves has put out a statement on X, confirming that she has not just been sacked as chancellor but is leaving government altogether. It has been speculated Reeves could have been demoted to a lesser role in cabinet. But Sky News is reporting she declined another role. The first ever female chancellor wrote on social media: double quotation mark It has been the privilege of my life to serve as the Chancellor of the Exchequer. The economy today is stronger, fairer and more resilient because of the choices we have taken as a Labour Government over the past two years. Stability restored, investment delivered and reform to our economy under way. I said when I was appointed Chancellor that I would judge my time in office if the lives ordinary working class people have been improved. I’m proud to say that they have. And to every young woman and girl let my time in office show there should be no ceilings on your ambitions, your hopes or your dreams. I wish the very best of luck to my successor, Andy and his cabinet. You have my full support, and I will continue to play my part in helping this Labour government deliver the change the country needs. Share 29m ago 10.38 EDT Steve Reed out as housing secretary Steve Reed, secretary of state for housing, communities and local government, has left government. The Starmer ally has posted a letter to new prime minister Andy Burnham on X. It reads essentially as a resignation letter but it is pretty clear Burnham has his own people to install in cabinet. He told Burnham in the letter that he will do “everything in my power to ensure you succeed”. View image in fullscreen Secretary of State for Housing, Communities and Local Government, Steve Reed, arrives at Downing Street on May 12, 2026 in London, England. Photograph: Carl Court/Getty Images Share Updated at 10.42 EDT 33m ago 10.34 EDT Burnham making 'pledges without any plan for delivery', say Tories The Conservatives said Andy Burnham was making “pledges without any plan for delivery” after he said his first policy as prime minister would be to end long-term rough sleeping across the UK. Shadow housing secretary James Cleverly said: double quotation mark In his speech in Downing Street, Burnham promised he would ‘go through that door behind me and issue my first instruction to end rough sleeping in our country’. But there is precious little detail on how this will be paid for. Burnham must come clean on what other MHCLG (Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government) programmes have been salami-sliced to fill the gap. In Manchester, Burnham promised to end rough sleeping, but failed because he didn’t do the hard work necessary. Now, again, he is making pledges without any plan for delivery. If he is really serious about government, he should lay out proposals cut welfare, fund defence and get Britain working again. View image in fullscreen Shadow housing secretary James Cleverly speaking to the media in Westminster, central London. Picture date: Wednesday April 8, 2026. Photograph: Aaron Chown/PA Share 43m ago 10.24 EDT Lisa O’Carroll Andy Burnham is the first prime minister of Catholic faith to take office in modern times. Boris Johnson was baptised a Catholic but converted to the Church of England while a young teenager, while Tony Blair converted to Catholicism after he left office. Archbishop Richard Moth, president of the Catholic Bishops’ Conference of England and Wales, has offered his congratulations to the new PM. Moth said: double quotation mark As president of the Bishops’ Conference, and on behalf of the Catholic community in our countries, I offer my warm congratulations to Andy Burnham on becoming leader of the Labour party and now prime minister. We are living in challenging times. As Christians we have a duty to be bridge-builders and peacemakers. Where there are tensions, hostility and injustice, we are called to respond with real concern and respect for the dignity of the person, for the needs of families, the marginalised, and the vulnerable. The prime minister faces many challenging issues as he begins his work, and I assure him and his family of my continued prayers for the task ahead. In a quick-fire interview in the Guardian in 2009, Burnham listed the three most important things in his life apart from family. “Everton FC, the Labour Party and the Catholic church - in that order. The reason I say that is, it all comes back to cultural identity,” he said. He told how his dad was worried about meeting his mother’s family for the first time as religion was so important in Liverpool then and he was a Catholic but his future wife was not. Share 45m ago 10.22 EDT Burnham speaks to president Trump - Sky News And there we have it, Andy Burnham has just spoken to US president Donald Trump on the phone. Sky News’ political editor Beth Rigby is reporting that the leaders had a call this afternoon and it was Burnham’s first call as prime minister. There is no readout of the conversation as yet but I’ll bring it to you as and when it becomes available. Share 47m ago 10.19 EDT Andy Burnham said he would tell Ukraine’s Volodymyr Zelenskyy he will “be there for him” and the UK’s support would remain “resolute” under his premiership. The new prime minister indicated that Zelenskyy would be one of the first foreign leaders he will speak to after replacing Keir Starmer in No 10. He also indicated he would have an early call with US president Donald Trump. He told broadcasters: double quotation mark Those will be my first calls, and I’ll be making very clear later today to president Zelenskyy that there is no change. I will be with him 100% as Keir Starmer has been, and Keir has done really quite an incredible job in not just backing Ukraine but being there in a personal way. And I will be absolutely there for him in the same way. I’ve worked with Ukrainian mayors over the last few years. I’ve built a network of support for Ukrainian mayors, I take all of that experience into my new role and president Zelenskyy will be left in no doubt whatsoever that Britain’s support is steadfast. It is resolute. It will be there for him and I will be there for him personally too. Share 52m ago 10.15 EDT DUP leader Gavin Robinson has questioned what new prime minister Andy Burnham “stands” for as he takes office. Robinson pointed out Burnham will be the seventh prime minister during his time as an MP. He welcomed indications that the digital ID scheme will be scrapped and the suggestion of drilling in the North Sea, but said he wanted to see encouragement of conditions for growth. “I think it’s clear across the country that people would like to see stability and leadership, and stability at the top, but I think many are asking the question today, ‘what does Andy Burnham stand for, what is his agenda for the United Kingdom’?” he told media in Belfast. “We know that there are 402 Labour members of parliament and none of the 402 were suitable either to go forward as a potential prime minister or even to have a contest and battle of ideas. “I don’t know what Manchesterism really means for the United Kingdom, and I don’t know what Burnhamism is either, so we will see.” Share 1h ago 10.10 EDT Tina McKenzie, policy chair of the Federation of Small Businesses (FSB), said: “We extend a warm welcome to Andy Burnham as he takes on the most important role in public life. This is a golden opportunity to restart the parliament, go for growth, and get it right on supporting small businesses. “The new prime minister has set out a positive, pro-small business stall in recent weeks, and we are optimistic that he can galvanise the new government to make these plans a reality. “That will mean reworking the mistakes on business rates made by the last government, by increasing small business rate relief, acting on costs after the sledgehammer of national insurance rises, and scrapping pointless paperwork that holds business back. “Where the last government did get things unquestionably right – such as the new late payment laws currently going through the Lords – we need the new prime minister to stand firm behind small firms.” Share 1h ago 10.00 EDT The Green Party has called for Andy Burnham to impose rent controls, take the water industry into public ownership and introduce wealth taxes. Green Party leader Zack Polanski said: double quotation mark We need action on sky high rents, with rent controls, we need to end the rip off and take water into actual public ownership, not tinkering with the existing failed model as is being suggested. We need wealth taxes as a first step towards tackling gross inequality and getting serious investment into our economy and public services. Share 1h ago 09.52 EDT Six highlights from Andy Burnham’s first Downing Street speech Peter Walker It clocked in at just over five minutes, and contained several themes and phrases familiar to Andy Burnham watchers. And yet there was quite a lot to the new prime minister’s first speech in Downing Street. My colleague Peter Walker has picked out some of the highlights for you here: Six highlights from Andy Burnham’s first Downing Street speech Read more Share

centerText: Andy Burnham becomes U.K. prime minister toggle caption Kirsty Wigglesworth/AP LONDON — Keir Starmer is out. Andy Burnham is in. On Monday, outgoing Prime Minister Starmer traveled to Buckingham Palace to tender his resignation to King Charles III. Shortly afterward, his successor, Andy Burnham, made the same journey and was invited by the king to form a new government. Burnham then returned to Downing Street, where he delivered his first remarks as prime minister. He said, "It requires my generation of politicians to raise our game." He said politicians are "acknowledging that many voters have grown frustrated with politics." "I want to be honest with you: We have not been good enough, and we need to be better. We will be," Burnham said. Burnham becomes the U.K.'s seventh prime minister in a decade, since Britons voted in June 2016 to leave the European Union. That decision ultimately shrank the British economy, triggered anger at the governing class and contributed to a revolving door of prime ministers. Sponsor Message Starmer exits after two years Starmer delivered his final speech outside No. 10 Downing St. earlier on Monday before heading to Buckingham Palace to formally resign as prime minister, declaring: "My work is done." "In 6 1/2 years, I took our party from a historic defeat in 2019, changed it so it was fit to face the country, and won a landslide general election victory in 2024," Starmer said. He described serving as prime minister as "the privilege of my life" and said he was leaving office confident Britain was "stronger and fairer" than it was two years ago. toggle caption Aaron Chown/Pool Photo via AP Closing his final address outside Downing Street, Starmer said, "I go with good grace, I go with a smile, and I go proud of everything we have achieved." Starmer led the U.K.'s center-left Labour Party to a landslide victory two years ago. But his personal approval ratings sank. Polls show voters believe he failed to deliver palpable change. He was also criticized for appointing Peter Mandelson, a close friend of the late sex offender Jeffrey Epstein, as British ambassador to Washington. Starmer later fired Mandelson. After Labour lost seats to a populist anti-immigration party in May's local elections, more of Starmer's own lawmakers joined calls for his removal. Sponsor Message Starmer resigned as Labour leader on June 22, and Burnham assumed that role on Friday. Under Britain's parliamentary system, the ruling party can replace its leader — and therefore the prime minister — without a general election. The next election is not expected for another three years. Burnham's first remarks as prime minister Burnham previously served as a member of Parliament for 16 years, holding several Cabinet roles in the Labour governments of Tony Blair and Gordon Brown. He ran twice for the Labour leadership, receiving a nomination from Starmer during the 2015 contest, before leaving Parliament to become mayor of Greater Manchester. As mayor, Burnham championed investment and devolution in the post-industrial region and has pledged to bring a similar approach to the U.K. nationally. He returned to Parliament in a special election last month. Standing outside Downing Street, Burnham said he will announce measures to help with the cost of living on Tuesday, promising immediate support for households while setting out plans for longer-term change. The new prime minister said his government would be "a circuit breaker for Britain, bringing forward the biggest changes in the last 40 years." "I can do something to give people some breathing space now, some help with the cost of living," Burnham said. He added, "I will soon go through that door behind me and issue my first instruction: to end rough sleeping in our country." Burnham also said he would unveil a 10-year plan for Britain later this year. A new prime minister, familiar challenges Born and raised in the grittier north of England, Burnham has cultivated a working-class hero vibe, often wearing T-shirts rather than suits and advocating for blue-collar workers. He moonlights as a DJ on Manchester's indie music scene. His social media videos have been compared to New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani's. Polls show he's one of the most popular U.K. politicians. Sponsor Message In terms of policy, Burnham has said he'll scrap plans for digital ID cards, reverse the privatization of public transit and utilities, and devolve power away from the capital, London, toward municipal and regional authorities. In recent days, there have been reports that Burnham might ease a ban on new drilling for oil and gas in the North Sea — which won him immediate praise from President Trump on social media on Sunday. Trump wrote that with North Sea drilling, Burnham would transform the U.K. from "a Poverty Stricken Disaster, to one of the Richest Countries anywhere in the World!" European leaders have begun congratulating Andy Burnham on becoming the U.K.'s new prime minister, with an invitation for his first foreign trip already arriving from Berlin. Ireland's Prime Minister Micheál Martin welcomed Burnham's appointment, saying he hoped to work with him to strengthen ties between the U.K., Ireland and the European Union. German Chancellor Friedrich Merz said Britain and Germany remained "close allies and partners," and invited Burnham to visit Berlin. But Burnham is likely to face some of the same challenges that stymied Starmer: rising costs of living, slow growth and that volatile American ally across the Atlantic.
centerRightText: NEW You can now listen to Fox News articles! farRightText: Veteran Westminster swamp creature turned ‘King of the North’ political chameleon Andy Burnham has been given the keys to Number 10 Downing Street, officially taking over the office of Prime Minister on Monday after Sir Keir Starmer was forced to resign just over two years after taking power. The London political churn has spat out another one. Sir Keir Starmer formally resigned as Prime Minister to make way for former Manchester Mayor Andy Burnham, who became the seventh prime minister in a decade, and fifth to take power in less than four years. While Burnham presents as a more personable figure than his predecessor, it remains to be seen how far northern charm will take him and if he can deliver on the key priorities of the British public. Starmer attempted to hold his head high on Monday morning as he left Downing Street — as opposed to the tearful resignation announcement he delivered last month — claiming that he left the country in a “stronger and fairer” position than it was when he came into office. This was clearly not the opinion of the British public, who delivered Starmer’s Labour Party an electoral drubbing in the local ‘mid-term’ elections in May in favour of Brexit boss Nigel Farage’s Reform UK party. The defeat came in the wake of his government failing to return the British economy to growth while imposing the highest tax burden on the nation since the Second World War and having failed to protect the country’s borders, with over 70,000 illegals crossing the English Channel under his watch, the most of any modern prime minister. The Starmer government was marred from the start, having bungled the response to the Southport massacre, in which he appeared to have more rage toward the so-called “far-right” rioters than towards the Rwandan-heritage teen who stabbed three young girls to death and left ten more injured at a Taylor Swift dance party just days into his term. However, the drumbeat call for his resignation truly ramped up last autumn following the release of the Epstein files by the Trump administration in the United States, which revealed that Starmer’s ambassador to Washington, Peter ‘Prince of Darkness’ Mandelson, had continued his close relationship with the New York financier long after he was convicted of his first child prostitution offence. Sir Keir attempted to limp on, throwing his longtime political ally, Chief of Staff, and architect of his rise, Morgan McSweeney, to the wolves. Yet his continued presence as Prime Minister appeared more of a function of no challenger being foolish to take over the job — and therefore the blame — before the expected heavy losses at the May elections, shortly after which self-described socialist Manchester Mayor Andy Burnham finally put himself forward for the top job. This morning, Burnham met with King Charles III for the traditional “kissing hands” ceremony, in which the British Monarch officially invited Burnham to form a government in his name. In his first address to the nation as Prime Minister outside Number 10, Burnham acknowledged the political turmoil that led to his rise, saying: “I am acutely conscious that I am… the seventh Prime Minister since 2016. Making this a moment for reflection, and new resolution… it requires my generation of politicians to raise our game, and rise to the challenge. Britain needs to show the world we can regain our stability once again.” In his speech, the new PM pitched himself as an economic reformer, promising to bring prosperity from London to the rest of the country. “We will take power out of here and carry it into every postcode in the land, so that they can do more, and in doing more, build a new economy where we put life’s essentials back under stronger public control to make them affordable to you again,” he said. Despite his folksy act, having taken the moniker of ‘King of the North’, following his nearly decade in office as the Mayor of Greater Manchester, Burnham appeared to have consciously concocted such an image after spending the prior decade as a Westminster insider under the governments of former Labour PMs Tony Blair and Gordon Brown. His rise to the highest office in the land over the past month also had the distinct odour of swamp. To put forward a leadership challenge against Starmer, Burnham needed a seat in the House of Commons, and the constituency of Makerfield was conveniently vacated by anti-Breitbart activist MP Josh Simons, who himself had been forced out of the Starmer government over scandals involving his pro-censorship activities at the Labour Together think tank founded by Starmer’s former Chief of Staff Morgan McSweeney. Simons, who appeared outside of Downing Street on Monday morning’s coronation for Burnham, is tipped to be set for a top gig in the new government in exchange for his move to give up his seat in Makerfield for the new PM. His return to power will likely signal continued crackdowns on social media, potentially including a ban on VPNs, as the government seeks to ban under-16s from accessing social media. Burnham’s victory in Makerfield last month was also facilitated, in part, by the help of the far-left cancel culture group HOPE Not Hate, which busied itself with digging up old social media posts of Burnham’s Reform opponent, and allegedly even leafleting on behalf of the Labour Party, for which Nigel Farage has launched a complaint at the Electoral Commission. Nevertheless, with just over 25,000 votes, Burnham was sent to Parliament and enabled to become Prime Minister. Yet, without having run on the 2024 Labour Party manifesto and only having been backed by a mere fraction of the public, Burnham is already facing calls for a general election from Farage and others, who argue that his administration has no popular mandate to govern. In the 1980s, Britain took some wrong turns. Political power was centralised, economic power privatised. Reindustrialising Britain again with public procurement to back British industry. Later this year I will bring forward a new Plan for Britain. A ten year plan.” Burnham vowed to bring down the cost of living, build more state housing, end homeless sleeping, reform the education system with more mental health support, and claimed that he would reduce welfare spending while meeting international defence commitments. Yet with details lacking on Monday and his new government already signalling against new energy extraction from the oil-rich North Sea, it remains to be seen how such an industrial plan will fare. topic: Taylor Farms False Positive Lettuce Cyclospora

left.title: FDA says review of Taylor Farms lettuce shows cyclospora finding was false positive

left-center.title: Taylor Farms lettuce sample was false positive for cyclospora, FDA says

right-center.title: FDA back to square one on source of cyclosporiasis outbreak after Taylor Farms false positive

farLeftText:
The US Food and Drug ⁠Administration said on Sunday that laboratory experts re-checked lettuce sample results from Taylor Farms in an ⁠investigation into an ⁠outbreak of the ​cyclospora parasite and concluded the finding should be considered a false positive.

As of Sunday, there were ⁠no confirmed positive sample results for cyclospora, the FDA added. The FDA on Saturday had said that a ⁠sample of shredded iceberg lettuce supplied by Taylor Farms tested positive ​for the parasitic illness that has ‌sickened thousands of ‌people in the US.

A spokesperson for Taylor Fresh Foods told Reuters ‌that the company was informed the FDA made a mistake.

“To be clear, at this moment, FDA has not identified a single positive product test result for cyclospora,” the company said. Taylor Fresh Foods added that it had completed a voluntary recall of iceberg lettuce ‌from central Mexico out of an abundance of caution based on the initial information given.

The FDA, in ​its latest update said it has “notified Taylor Farms and continues working with the firm to ensure the product implicated in this outbreak has been removed from the market”.

Taylor Farms has also said that the FDA apologized. The company had earlier confirmed that ⁠the product was not part of its current recall, according ​to the ​FDA.

Cyclosporiasis, which has been linked ​to shredded iceberg lettuce served at Yum Brands Taco ​Bell restaurants ‌in Indiana, Kentucky, Michigan, ​Ohio and ​West Virginia has so far resulted in around 100 hospitalizations and no deaths, according to the US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC).

The parasitic infection is caused by the single-cell protozoa cyclospora. The microbe is typically transmitted through water or food contaminated with feces. It is most common during warm weather months, from May to August.

It can take weeks or days for an infected person to develop symptoms, making it difficult to track cyclosporiasis. Diagnosis and reporting then takes weeks more.
centerLeftText:
A sample of lettuce from Taylor Farms that had tested positive for cyclospora was a false positive, the Food and Drug Administration said Sunday.

"Due to the complexity in detection of Cyclospora, FDA laboratory experts re-reviewed the sample results and have concluded that the finding does not represent true amplification and should be considered a false positive," the FDA said.

In the statement announcing the false positive, the FDA said that as of Sunday it had not found any "confirmed positive sample results for product testing for Cyclospora."

Taylor Farms on Friday said it was "voluntarily removing" iceberg lettuce sourced from central Mexico after the lettuce had been linked to one of the largest outbreaks of cyclosporiasis in the U.S. in recent years.

"While the FDA traceback is indicating a specific independent farm, which represents less than 1% of the U.S.'s iceberg lettuce supply, as the potential source of the outbreak, we have removed all iceberg lettuce from the region indefinitely," the company said Friday in a statement.

Taylor Farms said Sunday in a statement after the false positive was announced that, "Our thoughts remain with everyone who has fallen ill in this outbreak. We are committed to working with public health authorities as the ongoing outbreak investigation continues."

On Thursday, the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention identified lettuce supplied to select Taco Bell restaurants as a source of the multistate outbreak, more than a month after cases first began to appear.

The iceberg lettuce was distributed to Taco Bell restaurants in Indiana, Kentucky, Michigan, Ohio and West Virginia, according to the CDC. Taco Bell said on Friday it had "completed removal of affected Taylor Farms lettuce from our restaurants."

Walmart also announced over the weekend it had removed four Marketside bagged iceberg lettuce salad products from its stores in more than two dozen states, but added there was "no indication that products sold in our stores are affected by the current Cyclospora investigations."

The outbreak, first reported in May, has spread to 34 states and sickened more than 1,600 people, according to the CDC. However, the CDC's tally lags behind state health departments, which have reported much larger numbers of cases. For instance, the Michigan Department of Health and Human Services reported more than 5,000 confirmed cases as of Friday.

No deaths have been reported.

Cyclosporiasis is a food- and waterborne illness caused by the cyclospora parasite, which causes long-lasting, watery and sometimes explosive diarrhea, according to public health experts.

Outbreaks typically occur in late spring and summer and have historically been linked to bagged salads, cilantro, basil, raspberries, snow peas and green onions.

The investigation into the source of the cyclosporiasis outbreak is going back to the drawing board after the Food and Drug Administration said a sample of Taylor Farms shredded lettuce produced a false positive result.

Taylor Farms, a California-based produce farm and supplier, announced a voluntary 27-state recall of its shredded iceberg lettuce on Friday after officials linked the product to the outbreak. The FDA had come out with a positive cyclospora result for Taylor Farms’ iceberg lettuce on Saturday, but declared it a false positive the following day.

“Due to the complexity in detection of Cyclospora, FDA laboratory experts re-reviewed the sample results and have concluded that the finding does not represent true amplification and should be considered a false positive,” the FDA said in an update statement on Sunday evening.

topic: Spain Argentina Extra World Beat 2026

left.title: Spain beat Argentina 1-0 after extra time to win the 2026 World Cup – as it happened

center.title: PHOTOS: Spain wins the World Cup by beating Argentina 1-0 on Ferran Torres' goal in extra time

farLeftText: 15h ago 20.06 EDT Nick Ames It ended almost as Gianni Infantino and Donald Trump had planned. Earlier on a seemingly endless World Cup final day the trophy had, in a scene perfectly of a piece with the event’s excess, been let out of a Louis Vuitton trunk. Now they shuffled up to the podium together and presented it to a beaming Rodri, fumbling the moment slightly when Trump lingered for Spain’s team photo. Within moments they had made their exit, golden glitter sparkling on a bobbly pitch and the reverberations from a football event of unprecedented scale pulsing far beyond this concreted mini-state in New Jersey. How to make sense of a heady, intoxicating, often deeply strange tournament that at once appeared fulsomely welcoming and chillingly distant? It concluded with a disorientating fever dream beneath a bright blue American sky, football appearing a sideshow for much of the afternoon even if the game did not exactly help itself. Anyone straining for a parable could, if they wanted, take pleasure in Spain’s success. This was ultimately the triumph of a collective idea in a World Cup nurtured to indulge the cult of the individual. Trump show offers fitting fever-dream end to a World Cup of excess and damage Read more Share 15h ago 20.04 EDT More from Argentina manager Lionel Scaloni double quotation mark I feel sad. One could say many things about how we got here, but it’s not worth it. Endless thanks to these players who once again took us to the final. If they give everything the way they did today, it sets a great example for ‌the people and for our country. You have to pick yourself up again. When you leave everything like that, it’s very hard to find fault with anything.” [On Lionel Messi] He’s 39 years old now. It was crystal ​clear for me that he was going to play up until he decided not to. I hope that everyone feels proud of him, of what he’s achieved because he’s the best football player ever to set foot on a pitch. I have no doubt about this, and what he did during this World Cup was incredible. But also what he did before this. [On his own future] I will talk to the president. I believe it’s only ‌fair that I can ​take this time for myself ​to think this through. I never thought I would get to this place and it’s wonderful. In order to continue, you need a great deal of things, resetting your mind, rebooting, creating a group like this again – that is very difficult. I am very sorry. Share Updated at 20.05 EDT 15h ago 19.57 EDT Sam Jones Neither the approach of yet another enervating heatwave, nor the prospect of heading to work on Monday morning, was going to keep Madrileños huddled indoors when the World Cup, and the stifling streets of the capital, were there for the taking. With the temperature at 9pm nudging 32C, people across the city and the wider region filled bars, parks, fairgrounds and bullrings to drink, to stripe the streets red-and-yellow with their rojigualda flags, and to test their lungs, their nerves, and, as it turned out, their boredom thresholds. In the capital itself, the faithful descended on a giant screen by the river Manzanares and trooped into the cavernous concrete confines of the 15,000-capacity Movistar arena. Most, however, appeared to have made for the Plaza de Colón to watch the game under the statue of Christopher Columbus, for whom the square is named. Overhead, sharp against a bright blue July sky, flew the largest flag in Spain: a vexillological behemoth that measures 21m by 14m and weighs 35kg. In Madrid fans defy heat, early starts and the torpor of a slow game to cheer Spain to World Cup victory Read more Share 15h ago 19.53 EDT Charles De Ketelaere is the answer to a future quiz question. He was the only man to score against Spain at this World Cup in 12 hours of football. They never went behind, they were only taken to extra-time in the final. In the parlance of our times, they’re a beautiful, brilliant football team. Share 15h ago 19.44 EDT Lionel Scaloni’s reaction double quotation mark They (Spain) were better, that’s the truth. But I’ll hold on to an enormous memory of what they’ve done, of what it’s worth to get this far. We’re great in victory and we have to be great in defeat. Today we’re showing that we know how to lose. We lost the match and we’re owning it. But that doesn’t mean we’ll stop remembering everything we did to get here. Share 15h ago 19.33 EDT Rodri, a giant of a human being and a pretty good footballer too, reflects on Spain’s triumph and his comeback from injury double quotation mark I cannot express it with words. It’s like we’re in a cloud. It’s been a very tough time. I just want the new generations to see my example as an opportunity – when you go down you can rise again. This is my philosophy my entire life. Of course, sometimes things go well, things they go wrong. But always the positivity I said to the lads before the game – we have to go for the game. We need to face them. We have to look to the eyes and see it. We won this game and then life gives you the World Cup or not. But we were brave and I was brave in that moment. Share 15h ago 19.26 EDT Thanks to Francis Fowles for this cracking stat (assuming it’s correct) Only four players have won the World Cup, European Championship, European Cup/Champions League and Ballon d’Or: Franz Beckenbauer Gerd Muller Zinedine Zidane Rodri Share 15h ago 19.19 EDT Mikel Merino’s verdict double quotation mark Unbelievable. It’s the perfect moment for us – we’re such a golden generation with these young players who give everything for this country, for these fans. It was a game about concentration. We knew we were a better team in terms of football. The pitch was dry and they know how to make the game difficult to you. I think the team was outstanding today and, yeah, we deserved it. When you are facing a low block, you have to attack the box with bodies on the line and control the transitions. Luckily we have very good crossers of the ball, big threats in the box. That’s the old-school way of scoring goals and we did it. [What’s the superpower of this team?] The connection. The family we are. Share 15h ago 19.17 EDT Will Unwin ”I thought it was crap,” Wayne Rooney surmised on BBC after enduring the half-time show. Amid tense final, the interval was always going to be prominent, theoretically bringing light to the shade. Why the two UK broadcasters felt the need to transmit the karaoke session in full we will never know but at least the pundits suffered through it, too. Ally McCoist said it had “mentally scarred” him as someone in attendance, unlike many of his ITV colleagues who were sat in Brooklyn. The studio has rightly earned them kudos, creating a home alongside the Hudson River with the Brooklyn Bridge providing the dramatic backdrop. With the game only an expensive train ride away, it was a surprise to see Roy Keane, Gary Neville and Ian Wright in familiar surroundings. ‘It was crap’: Wayne Rooney’s candour helps BBC in World Cup final TV battle Read more Share 15h ago 19.15 EDT Alexander Abnos Donald Trump and Gianni Infantino were booed as they took to the field for the presentation ceremony of the World Cup final on Sunday. Trump arrived at the stadium on Marine One, with the president’s helicopter first flying over the stadium about 45 minutes before kick-off. There was little reaction from the crowd as the aircraft flew over the stadium, with Marine One landing nearby a few minutes later. The president was seated in a glassed-in section of luxury seats on the west side of the stadium shortly before kick-off, along with the Fifa president, Gianni Infantino. Other dignitaries and former players sat nearby, including Spain’s king, Felipe VI, Canada’s prime minister, Mark Carney, and Mexico’s president, Claudia Sheinbaum. Share 16h ago 19.09 EDT Ferran Torres' reaction double quotation mark It was ​a goal scored by 47 million ​people – it wasn’t even mine or from the 26 players. It was destined ​to happen; it was ‌meant to be ​a winner. Whilst ​all finals are tough, when you’re up against [Lionel] Messi, you do get a bit nervous. We played better football. I’ve been heavily criticised throughout the World Cup. Thank God, who always gives me the strength to carry on. God gives things to those who deserve them most. Share 16h ago 19.08 EDT Luis de la Fuente's reaction double quotation mark I’m so ‌proud of this generation of footballers who have grown up with this philosophy, remained true to ‌it, made it even better, and set an example as a team and a family – they’re excellent, world-class footballers with exceptional talent. I’m very moved. Looking back and thinking about them, we’ve won everything, absolutely everything, with this generation of players. The match should have been decided much earlier ... but this is a World Cup final and you have to dig deep even against ten men, and we’re prepared for anything. Share 16h ago 19.07 EDT “I actually thought it was a pretty decent game, as it goes,” says James Humphries. “A clash of styles, in that one team had one, and also some drama and some quality defending. Or has the game gone to such an extent that we think 6-4 is a serious scoreline?” Chill out Jose. Share 16h ago 19.06 EDT “Shout out to 2010 on the bad World Cup finals list?” says Tom Hopkins. “The Dutch did disgrace themselves in slightly more entertaining fashion that day though.” Fair point, but at least the villains (sic) had some genuine chances in that game. Off the top of my weary bald head, I’d say the worst men’s World Cup finals, in order, are probably 1990, 2026, 1994, 2014, 2010. Share 16h ago 19.03 EDT Jacob Steinberg A worrying thought as a tepid first half drew to a close: was the half-time show going to be better than the football? This was a slog. Argentina could not match Spain’s possession approach but they had ways to level the playing field. There was physicality from Lionel Scaloni’s side, whose aim was to kill the spectacle before taking control in the second half, and they were allowed to take it to the limit by the Slovenian referee, Slavko Vincic. Alexis Mac Allister could have been booked for an early foul on Dani Olmo and the leniency, together with a pitch not conducive to playing flowing football, allowed Argentina to disrupt Spain’s passing game, creating the conditions for one of the most tedious World Cup finals. Patience beats overreliance on Messi: how the final was won and lost Read more Share 16h ago 19.00 EDT “Where are we placing this one in the list of all time worst World Cup finals?” writes Kári Tulinius. “My first instinct is to put this second behind 1994, but at least that gave us the tragedy of Roberto Baggio’s penalty miss. I’m already struggling to remember anything about this game.” I’m not sure 1994 was as bad as we remember I can’t see 1990 ever being surpassed. Share 16h ago 18.57 EDT Roy Keane on Rodri double quotation mark He’s got the full package – his decision making, being an option for his team-mates, when to play one- or two-touch. He’s a big strong boy, helps out with set-pieces; he seems like a really top pro. If you’re talking about the greatest midfielders in the history of the game, he has to be up there. He turns up all the time. Every time he gets the ball, he makes the right decision. The guy’s amazing. Share 16h ago 18.51 EDT The World Cup roll of honour 5 Brazil 4 Italy, Germany 3 Argentina 2 Uruguay, France, Spain 1 England Share 16h ago 18.51 EDT Now, Spain players are crowding around a replica shirt with a difference – there are two stars above the crest. Time to hand you back to Rob Smyth for more reaction. Share 16h ago 18.48 EDT Rodri hoists the trophy aloft and the ticker-tape cannons go off. The World Cup is handed around players, then to manager Luis de la Fuente, before coaching staff are invited on to the podium en masse. Every person touching that gold trophy is fulfilling a lifelong dream – even the Spanish royal family look a touch overawed. View image in fullscreen Rodri lifts the trophy! Photograph: Bradley Collyer/PA View image in fullscreen Gianni Infantino carefully explains to Donald Trump that he is not part of the Spanish national team. Photograph: Javier García/Shutterstock Share Updated at 22.00 EDT 16h ago 18.45 EDT King Felipe VI, the king of Spain, is also in the queue of dignitaries lined up to congratulate the world champions. Trump tells Rodri “you’re my favourite player” – and then stops the Spain captain for another chat as he’s about to lift the trophy. Even Infantino looks embarrassed at this point, but Trump insists on standing alongside the Spain players, who eventually focus on the business of celebration. Share 16h ago 18.40 EDT Argentina go up and collect their silver medals, and it’s all too much for Lionel Messi. Having worn a downbeat, bemused expression since the final whistle, the tears now start to flow. It’s almost certain that the greatest player of all time has played his last World Cup match, and that’s sad whoever you support. View image in fullscreen Photograph: Sarah Yenesel/EPA Share Updated at 18.41 EDT 16h ago 18.39 EDT The Golden Boot goes to Kylian Mbappé, although he’s not here to receive any award in person. The France forward scored 10 goals, with Messi unable to overtake him in this year’s chart or the all-time standings today. Golden Boot: World Cup 2026 top goalscorers Read more Share 16h ago 18.37 EDT Rodri wins the Golden Ball And people thought he was over the hill. The Spain midfielder has been back to his metronomic, unflappable best at this World Cup, and a deserving winner. He gets a “well played” from Trump, and a trophy to go alongside his Ballon d’Or. Somewhere in Brazil, Vinícius Jr turns the telly off. View image in fullscreen Photograph: Ulrik Pedersen/NurPhoto/Shutterstock Share Updated at 18.40 EDT 16h ago 18.33 EDT Pau Cubarsí wins young player of the tournament The Barcelona centre-back gets the nod over teammate and clubmate Lamine Yamal, and it’s thoroughly deserved; Spain conceded one goal in the entire tournament. On that theme, Unai Simón has won the Golden Glove – and opts against a repeat of Emi Martínez’s antics in 2022 with the trophy. View image in fullscreen Pau Cubarsi receives the young player of the tournament award. Photograph: Franck Fife/AFP/Getty Images Share Updated at 18.35 EDT 16h ago 18.31 EDT I should also point out that the president of Mexico, Claudia Sheinbaum, and the Canadian prime minister, Mark Carney, are also here for the presentation. We’ll start with the referees, but now they’re handing out some individual prizes … Share 16h ago 18.29 EDT Donald Trump and Gianni Infantino make their way on to the pitch, to plenty of jeers and whistles from the crowd. On the BBC’s coverage, Wayne Rooney labels Infantino’s white trainers “stupid”, which I enjoyed. View image in fullscreen Gianni Infantino with his ‘stupid’ trainers and stable genius Donald Trump. Photograph: Steph Chambers/FIFA/Getty Images Share Updated at 18.51 EDT 16h ago 18.27 EDT We’re getting close to trophy time; there is a huge replica of the World Cup in the centre of the pitch, and now Sergio Ramos is bringing out the real thing. A perfect choice, Ramos: he’s Spanish but also loved a bit of skulduggery in his playing days. View image in fullscreen Sergio Ramos, surprisingly not being sent off while bringing the trophy onto the pitch. Photograph: Carl Recine/Getty Images Share Updated at 18.31 EDT 16h ago 18.18 EDT A replay of Ferran Torres’s goal shows that he was being tracked by Simeone, who then pulled up on the edge of the area with an injury. That gave Torres the space he needed to score. Share 16h ago 18.15 EDT That was pretty intense, so I’m going to take a break and hand over to Niall McVeigh for a bit. Share 16h ago 18.13 EDT Alexis Petridis Like hydration breaks and Donald Trump’s ability to get refereeing decisions overturned by simply phoning up the president of Fifa and complaining, the final half-time show is an innovation of the 2026 World Cup that comes with a degree of controversy attached. For one thing, its existence automatically means the World Cup final breaks the International Football Association’s 15-minute half-time mandate. For another, its obvious antecedent lies in the NFL’s Super Bowl, where the half-time entertainment has mushroomed over the decades from a few marching bands to one of the big pop events of the year. To its detractors, it smacks of America not so much embracing the World Cup as imposing itself upon it. Hits, misses and Muppets: the star-studded World Cup half-time show nobody asked for Read more Share 17h ago 18.11 EDT Modern sport, we’re told, is all about the individual. Your boy has 422 gazillion Instagram followers, blah blah blah. Spain and Paris Saint-Germain, the best teams in the world, beg to differ. The best team usually wins the World Cup, but rarely has the T-word been as operative in 2026. View image in fullscreen Photograph: Ronald Wittek/EPA Share Updated at 18.15 EDT 17h ago 18.08 EDT Matt Hughes Spain finally broke Argentina’s dogged resistance in extra time to win the men’s World Cup for the second time with a 1-0 victory. The Barcelona winger Ferran Torres scored the only goal of a disjointed game that was almost ruined by Argentina’s deliberate spoiling tactics, which backfired when they were reduced to 10 men for extra time by the late dismissal of Enzo Fernández for two yellow cards. Spain win the World Cup after Ferran Torres dethrones 10-man Argentina in extra time Read more Share 17h ago 18.07 EDT Paredes sent off after the final whistle As in 1990, Argentina’s attempt to defend their trophy has ended with two men being sent off in the final. Paredes could have been sent off several times during the game. One of the Argentina players punched a Spanish substitute in the stomach as well. Can you be given a retrospective red card for incidents after the final whistle? View image in fullscreen Genuinely pathetic from Leandro Paredes Photograph: Amanda Perobelli/Reuters Share Updated at 18.14 EDT 17h ago 18.06 EDT Spain have become the first team to hold the World Cup in both men’s and women’s football. Their triumph at this tournament brooks no argument. They’re the purest football team in the world and the best. It took them a long time to break down a fiercely resilient Argentina, but Ferran Torres assumed the Iniesta role by scoring the winning goal in the 106th minute. Ferran Torres Share Updated at 18.20 EDT 17h ago 18.03 EDT There’s some aggro in the middle of the pitch, and apparently Paredes is throwing punches. What a bedside manner. View image in fullscreen Gavi clashes with Argentina’s Leandro Paredes at full-time. Such terrible sportsmanship from Argentina. Photograph: Kai Pfaffenbach/Reuters Share Updated at 18.28 EDT 17h ago 18.02 EDT Full time: Spain 1-0 Argentina Spain are world champions! Share Updated at 18.28 EDT 17h ago 17.59 EDT 120+2 min: Great chance for Simeone! Messi’s near-post corner is only half cleared to Simeone, who is leaning backwards and shoots over from 10 yards! That’s Argentina’s best chance of the game by a mile. Share Updated at 18.38 EDT 17h ago 17.58 EDT 120+1 min Five minutes of added time. Blimey. Share 17h ago 17.58 EDT 120 min: Chance for Argentina! So close to an equaliser! After a short free-kick from Messi, Simeone got round the back and hammered a low cross that was brilliantly turned past the near post by Cubarsi. He had to make that. Share Updated at 18.38 EDT 17h ago 17.55 EDT 117 min Messi collects a bouncing ball 25 yards out and wallops a shot that smashes Merino straight in the coupon, knocking him off his feet. That’s Argentina’s first attempt at goal all night; their xG is now 0.04. Play is stopped while Merino is checked for concussion. Share Updated at 17.55 EDT 17h ago 17.54 EDT 116 min What the hell was that? Williams beats Molina on the left, moves into the area and tries to find Torres with a showboaty flick when a simple low pass would suffice. He makes a total mess of it and knocks the ball out of of a goalkick. If Spain don’t win the World Cup, he’ll regret that for the rest of his natural-born days. Share Updated at 17.58 EDT 17h ago 17.52 EDT 115 min Messi takes a short corner on the right, gets it back and curls a big, dipping cross that briefly startles Simon before landing on the roof of the net. Share Updated at 17.53 EDT 17h ago 17.52 EDT 115 min Ferran Torres is a very good player, not a great one. But he is a few minutes away from joining the best club in world football. These are the men who have scored the winning goal in a World Cup final: Angelo Schiavio, Alcides Ghiggia, Helmut Rahn, Gerd Muller, Jorge Burruchaga, Andreas Brehme, Andres Iniesta, Mario Gotze. Share Updated at 18.38 EDT 17h ago 17.51 EDT 114 min: Torres has a goal disallowed. It looked very tight as he ran through from the halfway line to beat Martinez, but the semi-automated technology confirmed the decision straight away. View image in fullscreen Ferran Torres is a fraction offside. Photograph: John Sibley/Reuters Share Updated at 17.59 EDT 17h ago 17.49 EDT 111 min Mac Allister is booked for a foul on somebody or other. Argentina look semi-resigned to their fate. Share 17h ago 17.47 EDT 110 min That was Spain’s 20th attempt at goal, 12 on target. Argentin- ah you know that. Share 17h ago 17.46 EDT 108 min If Argentina win the World Cup from here, they will go down as football’s greatest mentality monsters. Share

centerText: EAST RUTHERFORD, N.J. (AP) — Ferran Torres' first goal of this year's World Cup was the goal of his life, and it put Spain back atop the soccer world. Denying Lionel Messi a second straight title in what he has indicated would be his final World Cup match, Torres came off the bench and scored in the 106th minute and Spain — in a match it simply dominated — ended Argentina's reign with a 1-0 win on Sunday. Torres pounced on a bouncing ball in the box and used his left foot to bury a shot just under the crossbar and into the back of the net. "It was a goal scored by 47 million people," Torres said, referring to Spain's population. Maybe not by them — but certainly for them. It was Spain's second title, this trophy getting added to the one claimed in 2010. And there's no question about the center of the soccer universe now, not after this display. Spain allowed only one goal in eight matches at the tournament, setting a World Cup record for fewest goals allowed by a champion. And this title, combined with the World Cup won by Spain's women in 2023, makes the European nation the first to hold both the men's and women's titles simultaneously. "I feel sad," said Messi, who was moved to tears after receiving his runner-up medal and staring into the crowd at Argentina supporters. "They were better, to be honest." The score indicates this match was close, but only one team was in command. Spain took the game's first 20 shots on goal before Argentina — desperate for an equalizer — got its first attempt off in the second half of extra time. Spain took nine of the game's first 10 corner kicks, and only a World Cup final-record 12 saves by Argentina goalkeeper Emiliano Martinez gave the 2022 champions a shot at going back-to-back. Spain is now unbeaten in its last 38 matches — 29 wins, nine draws — and now is the outright holder of the longest such streak by any European team ever. Italy went 37 matches (28 wins, nine draws) from October 2018 through September 2021, before Spain ended that streak. And for the last two-plus years, nobody has beaten Spain. Not even Messi. An Argentina team that was the kings of the late-game rally in this tournament had no answer this time. Messi took a corner kick with about four minutes left before the final whistle and the ball took a fortunate skip toward reserve Giuliano Simeone — who sent it high and over the crossbar, holding his head in disbelief afterward. "When you have Messi on the other side, well, you are nervous," Torres said. "At the end of the day, we always depended on us. We always come up with our football, and we showed it again." The win made Spain coach Luis de la Fuente, at 65, the oldest coach ever to lead a team to the World Cup title. "We are world champions," de la Fuente said. "It was together we reached this stage." There was some pushing and shoving after the final whistle, which was quickly cleaned up and Spain commenced its celebration as most Argentina players sat on the turf in disbelief. Cooler heads prevailed, and Spain's players lined up minutes later to form an honor guard of sorts for Argentina's squad to walk through on its way to the stage for the runner-up medals. Enzo Fernandez was sent off in second-half stoppage time after getting his second yellow card, meaning Argentina was forced to finish the match with only 10 players. Fernandez was carded for a reckless play that sent Spain defender Pau Cubarsi cartwheeling into the air, and the card came out immediately. It was the 104th and last match of the biggest World Cup ever, a 48-team event that played out over the U.S., Canada and Mexico. Because there were more matches than any previous World Cup, it was no surprise that there were more goals than all other editions — more than 300, nearly three per match. Except, of course, when Spain was involved. Spain's plan was simple and flawless: Hold the ball as much as possible, denying opponents any hint of chances. It worked, all the way to the end. "There is sadness, but also the knowledge that we gave it our all," Argentina coach Lionel Scaloni said. "I could say a lot about how we got here, but it's not worth it. I am eternally grateful to these guys; they competed right to the end. The other team was better." Spain came into this tournament considered as one of the favorites, and with the full belief it could end Argentina's reign. It had to go through the game's greatest player — maybe ever — to make good on that promise. Messi is 39 and the math certainly suggests that the Inter Miami star is at the end of his international career. There have been hints — such as Messi himself declaring that a home World Cup qualifying match against Venezuela last year would be his final competitive contest for Argentina on his nation's soil. "I experienced a lot of things on this pitch, good and not so good," Messi said that night. "I'll take the good with me." In the Messi era, there was a lot of good. A lot of great, actually. There was the World Cup title in 2022, obviously topping the list. There was the Olympic gold medal at the 2008 Beijing Games. The two Copa América titles, the first in 2021 and the second in 2024. He won Argentina's player of the year award 16 times in a span of 19 years, first winning it as a teenager and continuing to win it all the way into his late 30s. He's a legend within the game and on all corners of the globe. But Spain was better Sunday. "I spoke to my players, and they said: 'Look, we've won everything, coach. We've won everything,'" de la Fuente said. "And that is beautiful." The spectacle was not limited to simply soccer. There was a halftime show, a first for FIFA. There were a ton of celebrities there from stage, screen and sports: Brad Pitt, Tom Cruise, Matt Damon, Cillian Murphy, Daniel Craig, Stephen Curry, Victor Wembanyama, Shai Gilgeous-Alexander, Jalen Brunson, Kevin Durant, Serena Williams, Carlos Alcaraz, David Beckham, Zinedine Zidane and dozens more. They came to see a show. So did 80,000 other people, not to mention perhaps as many as 2 billion watching around the globe. Spain didn't disappoint. No team has won back-to-back men's World Cups since Brazil in 1958 and 1962, and Spain kept Argentina from pulling off that repeat. "We are gracious in victory," Scaloni said, "and we must be gracious in defeat."

farLeftText: From 17m ago 10.48 EDT Hamas names Khalil al-Hayya as new overall leader Hamas has named Khalil al-Hayya as its new overall leader, according to a statement from the Palestinian militant ⁠group, succeeding Yahya Sinwar who was killed by the Israeli military in Gaza in October 2024. Hayya, ⁠the group’s exiled Gaza chief and top negotiator, is generally seen as more hardline than ⁠Khaled Meshaal, the head of the militant group’s office in exile and the other frontrunner for the leadership. Share 25m ago 10.41 EDT Lebanese army begins taking over 'pilot zones' in southern Lebanon, state department says The US state department has said the Lebanese army has began taking charge of security in three southern villages under so-called “pilot zone operations”. “Today, pilot zone operations began in the villages of Froun, Srifa, and Zawtar Al-Gharbiya in accordance with the Trilateral Framework and under the auspices of the Military Coordination Group for Lebanon,” the US state department said in a statement. The state department described the step as “a direct outcome” of talks held between Israeli and Lebanese delegations in Rome last week, adding that Washington would keep working with both sides “to implement the framework to a successful conclusion”. Lebanon and Israel announced a “framework agreement” in late June, laying out a plan for Israeli forces to withdraw from southern Lebanon, in exchange for the disarmament of Hezbollah, the Iranian-backed Lebanese militant group. View image in fullscreen Lebanon's president Joseph Aoun shakes hands with the US secretary of state, Marco Rubio, on 18 July in Washington, where he is expected to meet Donald Trump. Photograph: Lebanese Presidency/AFP/Getty Images The Lebanese president, Joseph Aoun, will reportedly present a plan in Washington to Donald Trump tomorrow on how to disarm Hezbollah and secure Israel’s withdrawal from southern Lebanon. Hezbollah has firmly rejected the government’s direct talks with Israel and efforts by the state, which it operates independently of, to strip it ​of its arms. As well as killing many Lebanese civilians in airstrikes across the country, the Israeli military has destroyed dozens of villages in the areas it occupies, something Human Rights Watch said could amount to a war crime. Hundreds of thousands of Lebanese people remain displaced from their homes. Israel has occupied large swathes of south Lebanon in what it calls a “security zone” and says it is trying to protect its northern communities from the threat of Hezbollah. Lebanon was drawn into the war when Hezbollah fired rockets at Israel on 2 March to avenge the US-Israeli killing of Iran’s former supreme leader in Tehran in late February. Share 40m ago 10.25 EDT Kuwait army says it is responding to Iranian attacks Kuwait’s army wrote in a post on X that it is “confronting” missile and drone attacks from Iran, adding that any loud explosions heard will be the sound of air defence systems intercepting aerial attacks. “Everyone is kindly requested to adhere to the safety and security instructions issued by the competent authorities,” it added. Iran’s Revolutionary Guard Corps reportedly said earlier that it hit US military targets in Kuwait, with the Fars news agency cited as saying the Ali Al Salem air base was targeted. The Kuwaiti army also earlier said its air defences were responding to hostile drones from Iran. Share 1h ago 09.43 EDT Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, speaker of Iran’s parliament, posted on X that the US keeps bringing new military equipment into the region while also claiming they want to end the war. A Saudi-led coalition imposed an air and sea blockade on Yemen in 2015, as part of its war efforts to dislodge the Houthis. The territory controlled by the Houthis in Yemen sits alongside the critical Bab al-Mandab strait, which connects the Red Sea to the Gulf of Aden and the Arabian Sea, and is vulnerable to Houthi attacks by drones missiles and small boats. US forces wrapped up its ninth consecutive night of strikes against Iran on Monday as the number of confirmed American military deaths in the renewed fighting rose to three. The strikes were aimed at “degrading” Iran’s ability to attack commercial vessels in the strait of Hormuz, the US military’s Central Command ​said . US ⁠missiles struck several Iranian cities early on Monday, according to Iran’s semi-official Tasnim news agency, with explosions heard in Tabriz, Chabahar, Konarak, Bandar Mahshahr and Bandar Imam Khomeini. Iranian foreign ministry spokesperson Esmaeil Baghaei on Monday called on the UN’s International Atomic Energy Agency to condemn the overnight US strikes on a nuclear power plant under construction in Darkhovin. Bahrain’s civil aviation authority said Iranian drones had targeted Bahrain’s air navigation system, but traffic has remained unaffected. Amid the exchange of strikes, diplomatic exchanges between the US and Iran have continued via mediators, said Iranian foreign ministry spokesman Esmaeil Baghaei. Though Baghaei said that Iran would not allow the strait of Hormuz to be used to threaten the country’s security – and that Iran was “determined to take the necessary measures” to safeguard its sovereignty – the news of the diplomatic talks was enough to bring the price of oil back below $90 a barrel. Donald Trump said the latest American strikes were being carried out in honour of the US service members killed in recent days. The US military had said a service member was killed on Saturday in northern ⁠Iraq during a controlled detonation on a downed Iranian drone, and that a day earlier two of its service personnel were killed in Jordan in an Iranian attack. On Monday, the US military identified the soldiers killed in Jordan as 25-year-old first lieutenant Tyler James Feehan of Ewa Beach, Hawaii and 19-year-old private Isabella Gonzales of Carrollton, Texas. Iran’s health ministry said on ‌Saturday that 50 people had been killed ​and more than 500 injured in US strikes ​on the country over the past three weeks. However, air navigation operations remained unaffected,” the civil aviation authority said in a statement, adding that “flights and air traffic” were proceeding normally.
